nativeRange.habitat: Clear lagoons and sheltered seaward reefs, typically along deep drop-offs; depth range 4–30 m. Juveniles are commonly found in seagrass beds.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on benthic invertebrates, sponges, molluscs, crustaceans, and algae in the wild. In captivity, tends to accept a varied meaty-foods diet including squid, krill, clams, mussels, and hard-shelled shrimp. Hard-shelled prey such as clams and crabs are generally advised to help wear down the species' continuously growing beak-like teeth.

summary: The Scribbled Arothron Puffer (Arothron mappa) is a very large Indo-Pacific species marked by a network of black 'scribble' lines over a tan-to-yellow body. It is generally considered semi-aggressive and best kept singly in an extremely large fish-only system with robust tankmates.
careNotes:
Arothron mappa is distributed throughout the Indo-Pacific from East Africa to Samoa, north to the Ryukyus and southern Japan, and south to New Caledonia and Queensland. Adults tend to inhabit clear lagoons and sheltered seaward reefs along deep drop-offs at depths of around 4–30 m; juveniles are commonly reported from seagrass beds. The species' body coloration ranges from light tan to dark yellow with an intricate network of black 'scribble' reticulations — most distinctive around the head and ventral surface — and a characteristic dark blotch around the anus.

Like other Arothron species, A. mappa has a beak-like structure formed by fused teeth that grow continuously throughout life; offering hard-shelled prey such as clams and crabs is generally advised to prevent dental overgrowth. The species lacks pelvic fins and uses its pectoral, dorsal, and anal fins for locomotion. Like all members of Tetraodontidae, it can inflate its body to nearly twice its normal size by ingesting water when threatened; inhaling air should be avoided as this can be fatal, so transfers are generally recommended using a container rather than a net.

The flesh, skin, liver, gonads, and intestines contain tetrodotoxin and are highly toxic if consumed. Although there is no documented evidence of A. mappa releasing skin toxins lethal to tankmates under stress (as in boxfishes), heavy filtration and a robust protein skimmer are generally advised given the species' large adult size and messy feeding habits.

Despite a semi-aggressive temperament rating, A. mappa is generally regarded as one of the milder large Arothron species and has been reported to coexist peacefully with other puffers and robust tankmates; conspecifics, however, are best avoided. It is not considered reef-safe and will consume sessile invertebrates, sponges, and crustaceans.

Because recommendations for minimum tank volume vary considerably — from around 1,140 L to 2,500 L depending on the source — aquarists are advised to provide as much volume and tank length as practical given the species' substantial adult size and need for open swimming space. A specific gravity in the range of 1.020–1.025 is broadly accepted for the species in captivity.
breedingNotes: Breeding has not been documented in captivity. The species is presumed to be a broadcast spawner in the wild like other tetraodontids, but no captive-breeding protocols are reported in the available literature.

additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Arothron mappa, Lesson 1831), taxonomy (Tetraodontidae / Tetraodontiformes), maximum length (65 cm TL), native range (Indo-Pacific, East Africa to Samoa), temperature range (25.4–29.3°C from wild data), depth range (4–30 m), habitat (clear lagoon and sheltered seaward reefs), and diet (algae, sponges, benthic invertebrates). Confirmed toxicity (poisonous to eat). IUCN Least Concern (2011).

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (65 cm / 25.6 in), minimum tank size (2,500 L), temperature (~26°C), salinity (~SG 1.026), temperament (mostly peaceful but may show aggression toward similar species), diet (larger and smaller crustaceans, invertebrates, plus tooth-maintenance foods), and reef safety (not reef-safe; threat to many invertebrates). Tank size of 2,500 L is significantly higher than LiveAquaria's 1,140 L; final value of 1,500 L sits between sources.

Confirmed family (Tetraodontidae), order (Tetraodontiformes), maximum length (~65 cm), distribution (Indian Ocean to western Pacific), habitat (deep drop-offs, clear lagoon and sheltered reefs, surface to 30 m; juveniles in seagrass), diet (benthic invertebrates, crustaceans, sponges, algae), and toxicity (tetrodotoxin in skin, liver, gonads, intestines; flesh can be safely consumed with specialized preparation).

fish.breedingNotes: Not yet documented in captivity. Presumed broadcast spawner in the wild.
